title: Elderflower skin wash for mild irritation
subtitle: A gentle infusion wash for dry, sensitive, or mildly irritated skin
excerpt: A cooled elderflower infusion used as a gentle skin wash for mildly irritated, dry, or sensitive skin. Elderflower is traditionally considered one of the gentlest herbs for topical use, with a long history in European domestic skin care.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, elderflower water as a traditional skin preparation for mildness and gentle cleansing. Traditional European use of elderflower water in domestic beauty and skin-care preparations, documented across 18th and 19th century household books.

content.text: Dried elderflower steeped in hot water, cooled, and used as a gentle

for mildly irritated, dry, or sensitive skin. Elderflower water is one of the oldest European home skin preparations. Gentle, fragrant, and long used as an everyday wash. At the strength used here, a single cup of brewed water on the skin, there are no real safety concerns for most people. The wash soothes the surface of the skin without stripping it.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Eczema: elderflower skin wash is a supportive measure for very mild skin irritation. Eczema requires proper medical management. A herbal wash may soothe mild flares on the surface but does not treat the underlying condition.

See your doctor for: eczema that is worsening, cracking, weeping, infected, or covering a large area of the body; any new skin rash in a child that is spreading or accompanied by fever; or skin symptoms that do not improve after one week of self-care.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Water
items.prepNote: just boiled, to be cooled before use on the skin

content.text: Place {{dried-elderflowers}} of dried elderflowers in the teapot. Pour {{water}} of just-boiled water over and lid immediately. Steep for 10 to 15 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a clean bowl. Allow to cool to room temperature. Apply with a clean cotton pad, cotton wool, or by splashing gently onto the affected skin. Pat dry with a soft, clean towel. Do not rub.

Apply once or twice daily to the irritated skin area. The elderflower wash can be used daily for as long as needed on mild irritation, there are no safety concerns with regular topical use at this strength. Keep each batch fresh; the infusion keeps refrigerated for up to 24 hours.
When NOT to use this remedy
Do not use on broken, weeping, or infected skin. See your doctor for skin that is cracking, weeping, spreading, infected, or not responding to self-care measures after one week.
